| Iron (Fe) | 1811 | 1300 – 1600 | 0.72 – 0.88 |
| Titanium (Ti) | 1941 | 1350 – 1750 | 0.70 – 0.90 |
| 316L Stainless Steel | 1700 | 1200 – 1500 | 0.71 – 0.88 |
| Tungsten Carbide (WC) | 3143 | 2200 – 2800 | 0.70 – 0.89 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram of Powder Metallurgy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Metal / Ore] --> B[Powder Production]
    B --> B1[Atomization]
    B --> B2[Mechanical Milling]
    B --> B3[Chemical Reduction]
    B --> B4[Electrolytic Method]
    B --> C[Powder Characterization]
    C --> D[Blending with Lubricant]
    D --> E[Die Compaction]
    E --> F[Green Compact]
    F --> G[Sintering Furnace]
    G --> H[Sintered Component]
    H --> I[Secondary Operations]
    I --> I1[Sizing]
    I --> I2[Impregnation]
    I --> I3[Heat Treatment]
    I --> J[Finished Biomaterial Part]
```

## 4.2 Die Compaction Mechanism Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Upper Punch] -->|Force F1| D[Die Cavity with Powder]
    D -->|Reaction Force| P2[Lower Punch]
    D --> M[Green Compact Forms]
    subgraph Compaction Process
        D
    end
    M -->|Ejection| E[Ejected Green Part]
    E --> S[Sintering Stage]
    S --> FN[Finished Sintered Part]
```

## 4.3 P/M Functional Architecture for Biomedical Implants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Titanium Powder] --> M1[Mixing]
    R2[Hydroxyapatite Powder] --> M1
    R3[Polymer Binder] --> M1
    M1 --> CP[Uniaxial Compaction 600 MPa]
    CP --> GC[Green Compact Porosity 35 percent]
    GC --> SN[Sintering at 1250 C in Argon]
    SN --> PI[Porous Ti HA Implant]
    PI --> APP[Application in Orthopedics]
    PI --> APP2[Application in Dental Implants]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1:** Define Powder Metallurgy. List any four advantages of the P/M process.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer:**
Powder Metallurgy is a manufacturing process in which fine metallic or ceramic powders are compacted and sintered below the melting point to produce finished or near-net-shape components.

**Advantages:**
1. Minimal material wastage (high material utilization > 95%).
2. Excellent dimensional accuracy and surface finish.
3. Capability to produce controlled porosity (vital for bone implants).
4. Suitability for fabricating parts from refractory and difficult-to-machine materials.

---

### **Question 2:** What is sintering? Why is it performed below the melting point?
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer:**
Sintering is a thermal process in which a green compact is heated to a temperature below the melting point of the constituent powder to develop metallurgical bonds through atomic diffusion.

**Reason for Sub-melting Operation:**
- Prevents loss of shape and dimensional accuracy.
- Allows retention of the porous structure (essential for biomaterial implants).
- Avoids reactions with crucibles and contamination from the molten state.
- Reduces energy consumption compared to full melting.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> A common mistake students commit is writing **stages of P/M** without explaining the **scientific reason behind each stage**. Examiners in KTU 2024 Scheme specifically award marks for the *scientific rationale* (e.g., *why* sintering is sub-melting, *why* lubricant is added). Merely listing stages fetches only 3–4 out of 14 marks. Always link each process step to the **purpose** and **mechanism**.

---

### **Question A (Choice 1):**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the various methods of metal powder production. Compare the characteristics of powders produced by atomization and mechanical crushing.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Discuss the stages of compaction and sintering in powder metallurgy with suitable diagrams. Explain how porosity is controlled during the process.

#### **Solution to Question A:**

##### **Part (a) Solution:**

The major methods of powder production are:

**(i) Atomization:** A high-pressure gas or water jet disintegrates a stream of molten metal into fine droplets that solidify as spherical powders. It is the most widely used industrial method. Particle sizes range from **10 µm to 300 µm** with excellent flow characteristics.

**(ii) Mechanical Crushing/Milling:** Brittle or ductile metals are mechanically pulverized using crushers, ball mills, or attrition mills. Particles are irregular in shape and have lower flowability. Suitable for hard, brittle materials like tungsten carbide.

**(iii) Chemical Reduction:** Metal oxides are reduced using reducing gases (e.g., $\text{H}_2$, CO) to yield fine metallic powders. Common for iron, copper, and tungsten.

**(iv) Electrolytic Deposition:** Metal is electrolytically deposited at the cathode and subsequently scraped off as a powder. Used for high-purity copper and iron powders.

**(v) Chemical Precipitation:** Metal salts are precipitated as hydroxides or oxalates, filtered, dried, and calcined.

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Atomization | Mechanical Crushing |
|---|---|---|
| Particle Shape | Spherical | Irregular / Angular |
| Flowability | Excellent | Poor |
| Purity | High | May include mill contamination |
| Particle Size Range | 10 – 300 µm | 1 – 500 µm |
| Cost | Moderate | Low |
| Best for Biomaterials | Yes (Ti, Co-Cr) | Limited (WC-Co) |

##### **Part (b) Solution:**

**Compaction Stage:**
The powder is poured into a rigid die and compressed uniaxially using upper and lower punches at pressures ranging from **400 MPa to 800 MPa**. The applied pressure causes:
- Particle rearrangement.
- Plastic deformation at contact points.
- Mechanical interlocking — giving rise to "green strength."

**Sintering Stage:**
The green compact is heated in a controlled-atmosphere furnace to **(0.7 – 0.9) Tm**. The driving force is the **reduction in surface free energy**. Atomic diffusion mechanisms (volume diffusion, grain boundary diffusion, surface diffusion) lead to neck growth between adjacent particles.

**Porosity Control:**
1. By varying compaction pressure (higher pressure → lower porosity).
2. By adjusting sintering temperature and time.
3. By adding pore-forming agents (e.g., ammonium bicarbonate) — particularly useful for bone implants requiring **30%–60% porosity**.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2):**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What are the key characteristics of metal powders used in P/M? Explain the significance of particle size, shape, and flowability.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the powder metallurgy process for the production of porous titanium implants used in orthopedics. Why is porosity important for these implants?

#### **Solution to Question B:**

##### **Part (a) Solution:**

The critical characteristics of metal powders are:

1. **Particle Size and Size Distribution:** Measured by sieve analysis or laser diffraction. Finer powders provide higher surface area, better sintering, and finer grain structure. Biomedical applications typically use powders of **45 µm – 150 µm**.

2. **Particle Shape:** Spherical powders flow better and pack uniformly; irregular shapes provide better green strength due to mechanical interlocking.

3. **Flowability:** Determines how easily the powder fills the die cavity. Measured by **Hall Flow Meter** as the time (seconds) for 50 g of powder to flow through a calibrated orifice.

4. **Apparent and Tap Density:** Apparent density is the loose fill density, while tap density is achieved after mechanical vibration.

5. **Compressibility:** The ability of powder to densify under applied pressure.

6. **Green Strength:** Mechanical strength of the compacted part before sintering.

##### **Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Powder Selection:** Commercially pure (CP) titanium or Ti-6Al-4V powder is produced via gas atomization (spherical particles, ~100 µm).

**Step 2 — Blending:** Powder is mixed with **1% zinc stearate** as a lubricant to minimize die-wall friction.

**Step 3 — Compaction:** Uniaxial pressing at **400–600 MPa** produces a green compact with ~30%–40% porosity.

**Step 4 — Sintering:** Heat treatment at **1200 °C – 1300 °C** in **high-purity argon atmosphere** for 2 hours.

**Step 5 — Secondary Operations:** Machining (if needed), surface treatment, and sterilization.

**Importance of Porosity in Orthopedic Implants:**
- Provides **mechanical interlocking** between implant and host bone.
- Facilitates **bone ingrowth (osseointegration)** by allowing vascularization.
- Mimics the **elastic modulus** of cancellous bone (~0.5 – 5 GPa), reducing **stress shielding**.
- Allows **transport of body fluids** and nutrients.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Powder Metallurgy**

- ✔ **Definition:** Powder Metallurgy = Compaction + Sintering (without melting).
- ✔ **Four Stages:** Powder Production → Blending → Compaction → Sintering.
- ✔ **Atomization** = Spherical powders; **Mechanical Crushing** = Irregular powders.
- ✔ **Compaction Pressure Range:** 400 – 800 MPa (for metals).
- ✔ **Sintering Temperature:** **(0.7 – 0.9) × Tm** (in Kelvin).
- ✔ **Sintering Atmosphere:** Inert (Ar, N₂) or Reducing (H₂) — never air for reactive metals like Ti.
- ✔ **Driving Force of Sintering:** Reduction in **surface free energy**.
- ✔ **Lubricant Function:** Reduces die-wall friction (e.g., zinc stearate, 0.5–1.5 wt%).
- ✔ **Green Compact:** Pre-sintered part with sufficient "green strength" to handle.
- ✔ **Porosity Control:** Done via compaction pressure, sintering time/temperature, and pore-formers.
- ✔ **Biomedical Relevance:** Porous Ti, Co-Cr-Mo implants require controlled porosity for osseointegration.
- ✔ **Heckel Equation:** $\ln \dfrac{1}{1 - D} = K \cdot P + A$ — describes compaction behavior.
- ✔ **Common Powder Tests:** Sieve analysis, Hall flowmeter, apparent/tap density measurement.

> **Final Exam Tip:** Always mention the **purpose** behind each process step. Examiners reward reasoning, not rote memorization.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Additive Manufacturing (Rapid Prototyping) Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT602 - BIOMATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Additive Manufacturing (Rapid Prototyping) Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Additive Manufacturing (AM)**, also historically termed **Rapid Prototyping (RP)** or **Solid Freeform Fabrication (SFF)**, is defined by the **ASTM F2792** standard as:

> *"A process of joining materials to make objects from 3D model data, usually layer upon layer, as opposed to subtractive manufacturing methodologies."*

In the context of **Biomaterials (PCBMT602)**, Additive Manufacturing refers to a suite of computer-aided fabrication techniques that build patient-specific (customized) scaffolds, implants, prosthetics, and surgical guides by sequentially depositing or solidifying biomaterial layers — typically **biopolymers (PLGA, PCL, PLA), bioceramics (HA, TCP), biometals (Ti alloys, Co-Cr), and hydrogels** — under digitally controlled conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Terminology — Memorize These Exact Terms:**
> - **Additive Manufacturing (AM)** — Current preferred ASTM term
> - **Rapid Prototyping (RP)** — Original term (1980s)
> - **Solid Freeform Fabrication (SFF)** — Academic term
> - **3D Printing (3DP)** — Popular/colloquial term
> - **Layer-by-Layer Manufacturing** — Descriptive phrase
> 
> Examiners give full credit ONLY when ASTM-standard terminology is used.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a sandcastle — but instead of shaping wet sand with a mold, you deposit **one thin horizontal slice of "magical sand" at a time**, and each slice instantly hardens exactly where the next one needs to stick. After hundreds of such slices stacked vertically, you have a complete 3D sandcastle of *any* complex shape, with internal archways, curved towers, and hollow chambers that would be **impossible** to carve out from a solid block.

**That is precisely how Additive Manufacturing works:**

- The **"magical sand"** = biomaterial (polymer melt, photocurable resin, powder bed, etc.)
- The **"horizontal slices"** = cross-sectional layers (typically **50 µm – 500 µm** thick) extracted from a **STL (Standard Tessellation Language)** digital file
- The **"instant hardening"** = the layer-solidification mechanism (UV curing, laser sintering, extrusion cooling, binder jetting)
- The **"sandcastle shape"** = patient-specific implant derived from **CT/MRI DICOM data**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in Biomaterials:**
> Traditional (subtractive) manufacturing of implants produces **generic shapes** that surgeons must manually carve intra-operatively to fit the patient's defect — leading to long surgery times, poor fit, and revision rates up to **20–30%**. AM enables **anatomically precise, patient-matched implants** directly from the patient's CT/MRI scan, with controlled internal porosity for **osseointegration** and **tissue ingrowth**.

## 1.3 Historical Evolution (Frequently Asked in KTU)

| Decade | Milestone | Significance |
|--------|-----------|--------------|
| 1984 | Charles Hull patents **Stereolithography (SLA)** | Birth of RP |
| 1988 | Carl Deckard patents **Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)** | Powder-based AM |
| 1989 | Scott Crump patents **Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM)** | Polymer extrusion AM |
| 1990s | Rise of biomedical applications | Custom implants, surgical planning |
| 2000s | Bioprinting research begins | Cell-laden hydrogel deposition |
| 2010s | Metal AM (EBM, DMLS) for orthopedics | Titanium acetabular cups, spinal cages |
| 2020s | Multi-material & 4D bioprinting | Shape-memory scaffolds, organoids |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Frequently Asked Datum:**
> *"Who invented 3D printing?"* → **Charles W. Hull (Chuck Hull), 1984, Stereolithography (SLA)**, co-founder of **3D Systems Corporation**. This is a **3-mark short-answer favorite**.

## 1.4 Core Principles — The Layer-by-Layer Philosophy

The fundamental paradigm shift is from **"Material Removal" (Subtractive)** to **"Material Addition" (Additive)**.

$$\text{Subtractive} : \text{Block} \xrightarrow{\text{Cutting/Milling}} \text{Part} + \text{Waste}$$

$$\text{Additive} : \text{Digital 3D Model} \xrightarrow{\text{Slicing}} \text{Layers} \xrightarrow{\text{Joining}} \text{Part (near-net shape)}$$

The generic AM workflow consists of **7 sequential stages** — examiners love asking this:

1. **CAD Model Creation** (or reverse engineering from CT/MRI)
2. **STL File Conversion** (triangulated mesh export)
3. **Slicing** (layer thickness, infill, support parameters)
4. **Build Setup** (orientation, support structures, material loading)
5. **Layer-by-Layer Fabrication** (the actual AM process)
6. **Post-Processing** (support removal, curing, sintering, sterilization)
7. **Quality Assurance & Application** (dimensional accuracy, biocompatibility testing)

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 ASTM F2792 Classification of AM Processes (HIGH-YIELD)

The **ASTM F2792** standard classifies ALL additive manufacturing processes into **7 broad categories**. This is the **most-tested topic** in KTU board exams for AM.

| Category | Acronym | Process Name | Energy Source | Typical Materials |
|----------|---------|--------------|---------------|-------------------|
| **Vat Photopolymerization** | VP | Stereolithography (SLA), Digital Light Processing (DLP) | UV Laser / Projector | Photopolymers, hydrogels, bio-resins |
| **Material Extrusion** | ME | Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM), Direct Ink Writing (DIW) | Thermal / Pneumatic | PCL, PLA, PLGA, hydrogels, bioinks |
| **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** | PBF | Selective Laser Sintering (SLS), Selective Laser Melting (SLM), Electron Beam Melting (EBM) | Laser / Electron Beam | Ti alloys, Co-Cr, PEEK, PA12, HA powders |
| **Material Jetting** | MJ | PolyJet, Inkjet Printing, NanoParticle Jetting | Thermal / Piezoelectric | Photopolymers, wax, bioinks, drug-loaded droplets |
| **Binder Jetting** | BJ | 3D Printing (3DP), Powder Bed Binder Jetting | Liquid binder droplet | Plaster, sand, metal + binder, ceramic + binder |
| **Sheet Lamination** | SL | Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM), Ultrasonic Additive Mfg (UAM) | Laser cut / Ultrasound | Paper, metal foils, ceramic tapes |
| **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** | DED | Laser Engineered Net Shaping (LENS), Electron Beam Additive Mfg (EBAM) | Laser / Plasma / E-beam | Ti, Co-Cr, Inconel, functionally graded materials |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Recall:** *"**V**ery **M**any **P**eople **M**ake **B**eautiful **S**culptures **D**irectly"*
> → **V**at, **M**aterial extrusion, **P**owder bed, **M**aterial jetting, **B**inder jetting, **S**heet lamination, **D**irected energy deposition.

## 2.2 Detailed Process Analysis — The Major Biomedical AM Technologies

### 2.2.1 Stereolithography (SLA) — Vat Photopolymerization

**Working Principle:**
A vat of **photopolymer resin** is selectively cured by a **UV laser** (typically **λ = 355 nm, HeCd or solid-state**). The laser traces each cross-section, polymerizing the liquid resin into a solid layer. The build platform lowers by one layer thickness, a recoater blade sweeps fresh resin, and the next layer is cured.

**Key Chemical Reaction (Free-radical photopolymerization):**
$$\text{Monomer (e.g., PEGDA)} + h\nu \xrightarrow{\text{Photoinitiator}} \text{Crosslinked Polymer Network}$$

**Mechanism steps:**
- **Initiation:** Photoinitiator (e.g., **Irgacure 819**, **Irgacure 2959**) absorbs UV photon, generates free radical.
- **Propagation:** Radical attacks C=C double bond of (meth)acrylate monomer, chain grows.
- **Termination:** Two radicals combine, chain stops.

**Biomedical Applications:**
- **Dental crowns & bridges** (resin-based)
- **Hearing aid shells** (custom-fit, biocompatible resin)
- **Surgical guides** (orthognathic, dental implant placement)
- **Tissue engineering scaffolds** (using PEGDA, GelMA photopolymers)
- **Microfluidic devices** for organ-on-chip

**Advantages:**
- High resolution (**≤ 25 µm** XY, **≤ 10 µm** Z)
- Excellent surface finish (Ra ≤ 1 µm)
- Transparent parts possible (useful for cell observation)

**Limitations:**
- Limited to photopolymers (not directly metals/ceramics)
- Requires **post-curing** (UV oven) for complete polymerization
- Biocompatibility depends on **residual monomer & photoinitiator** leaching
- Support structures required for overhangs

---

### 2.2.2 Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) — Material Extrusion

**Working Principle:**
A **thermoplastic filament** is fed into a heated liquefier (typically **180 °C – 250 °C**), extruded through a fine nozzle (diameter **0.1 – 0.4 mm**), and deposited along the toolpath defined by the slice. The material solidifies by cooling on the previously built layer.

**Critical Process Parameters:**
- **Nozzle temperature** (T_n)
- **Bed temperature** (T_b)
- **Layer thickness** (h)
- **Print speed** (v)
- **Infill density** (%)

**For Biopolymers (KTU favourite data):**

| Biomaterial | T_n (°C) | T_b (°C) | Glass Transition T_g (°C) | Application |
|-------------|----------|----------|---------------------------|-------------|
| PLA (Poly-Lactic Acid) | 190–220 | 50–60 | **55–60** | Bone scaffolds, drug delivery |
| PCL (Poly-ε-Caprolactone) | 80–120 | 30–45 | **−60** | Long-term implants, tissue engineering |
| PLGA (Poly Lactic-co-Glycolic Acid) | 100–150 | 35–50 | **45–55** | Controlled drug release |
| PEEK (Poly-Ether-Ether-Ketone) | 360–400 | 200–250 | **143** | Load-bearing spinal implants |
| ABS (Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene) | 220–250 | 100–110 | **105** | Surgical models, NOT implants |

**Biomedical Applications:**
- **Bone tissue engineering scaffolds** with controlled porosity
- **Surgical planning models** (anatomical replicas)
- **Drug delivery devices** (controlled-release matrices)
- **Customized orthotics and prosthetics**

**Advantages:**
- Low cost, simple, office/desktop use
- Wide material range
- Multi-material printing possible (dual extrusion)

**Limitations:**
- Lower resolution (100–200 µm typical)
- Layer-line artifacts (anisotropic mechanical properties)
- Thermal degradation of sensitive biopolymers
- **Mechanical strength is direction-dependent**

---

### 2.2.3 Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) — Powder Bed Fusion

**Working Principle:**
A **CO₂ laser (λ = 10.6 µm)** or **fiber laser (λ = 1.06 µm)** selectively sinters (heats just below melting point, **0.7–0.9 T_m**) polymer or metal powder particles in a powder bed. After each layer, a roller spreads a fresh powder layer (typically **100–150 µm** thick), and the process repeats.

**Critical Distinction (KTU exam favorite):**
- **Sintering** = heating below T_m → particles fuse but remain solid (no full melting)
- **Melting** (as in SLM) = heating above T_m → complete fusion (denser parts)

**Biomedical Materials in SLS:**
- **Polyamide (PA12, Nylon-12):** Surgical models, NOT long-term implants
- **PEEK:** Spinal cages, cranial implants (biocompatible, radiolucent, autoclave-stable)
- **Titanium alloys (Ti-6Al-4V):** Acetabular cups, spinal interbody fusion devices
- **Hydroxyapatite (HA) / TCP:** Bone scaffolds (often with polymer binder)

**Advantages:**
- **No support structures needed** (unsintered powder acts as support)
- Good mechanical properties (near-isotropic)
- Suitable for complex internal geometries

**Limitations:**
- Rough surface finish (powder adhesion)
- Porosity (typically **20–35%** — needs infiltration)
- High equipment cost
- Powder handling hazards (Ti powder = fire risk)

---

### 2.2.4 Electron Beam Melting (EBM) — Powder Bed Fusion (Metals)

**Working Principle:**
A **high-power electron beam** (typically **3–15 kW, accelerating voltage 60 kV**) in **high vacuum (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ mbar)** selectively melts metal powder layer by layer. The electron beam is steered by **electromagnetic coils** (not mirrors).

**EBM vs. SLM (KTU exam comparison favorite):**

| Parameter | EBM | SLM |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Energy source | Electron beam | Fiber laser (Yb-fiber, 1070 nm) |
| Atmosphere | High vacuum | Inert gas (Ar or N₂) |
| Power | 3–15 kW | 200 W – 1 kW |
| Build speed | Faster (high power) | Slower |
| Residual stress | Lower (hot build ~700 °C) | Higher (thermal gradients) |
| Surface finish | Rougher | Smoother |
| Typical materials | Ti-6Al-4V, Co-Cr | Ti, Co-Cr, Al, stainless steel |
| Medical use | Acetabular cups (ARCLINE by Arcam) | Spinal cages, dental implants |

**Biomedical Applications:**
- **Ti-6Al-4V acetabular cups** for hip replacement (Smith & Nephew, Exactech)
- **Spinal interbody fusion cages**
- **Craniomaxillofacial (CMF) plates**
- **Customized tumor resection prostheses**

---

### 2.2.5 Three-Dimensional Printing (3DP) — Binder Jetting

**Working Principle:**
A **liquid binder** is jetted from a **thermal or piezoelectric printhead** onto a powder bed, selectively binding particles together to form each layer.

**Biomedical Use — Drug Tablets & Tissue Scaffolds:**
- **ZipDose® Technology (Aprecia Pharmaceuticals):** First FDA-approved 3D-printed drug — **Spritam® (levetiracetam)** for epilepsy (2015).
- **Custom-release multi-drug tablets** with complex internal architectures.
- **Ceramic bone scaffolds:** HA powder + binder + sintering post-process.

---

### 2.2.6 Bioprinting (Emerging KTU Topic)

A specialized subset of AM where **cell-laden bioinks** (cells suspended in hydrogel precursors like **alginate, GelMA, collagen, fibrin**) are deposited in a layer-by-layer manner to fabricate **3D tissue constructs**.

**Three major bioprinting techniques:**

| Technique | Mechanism | Cell Viability | Resolution |
|-----------|-----------|----------------|------------|
| **Extrusion-based** | Pneumatic/piston extrusion of bioink filament | **40–80%** (high shear) | 100 µm – 1 mm |
| **Inkjet (droplet)** | Thermal/piezo droplet ejection | **>85%** (gentle) | 50–300 µm |
| **Laser-assisted (LIFT)** | Laser-induced forward transfer | **>95%** (nozzle-free) | 10–100 µm |

**KTU Note:** The *inverse relationship* between **resolution** and **cell viability** is a frequently tested concept. Extrusion = good mechanical integrity but shear damages cells; Inkjet = gentle but low viscosity limits; Laser = best viability but very slow & expensive.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Variables & Units | KTU Use |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------------------|---------|
| 1 | **Volumetric Build Rate (VBR)** | $\text{VBR} = A_{\text{layer}} \times h \times f_{\text{recoat}}$ | A_layer (mm²), h = layer thickness (mm), f_recoat = recoating frequency (Hz) | Compare AM process productivity |
| 2 | **Layer Thickness (h)** | $h = \dfrac{\Delta z_{\text{build}}}{N_{\text{layers}}}$ | Δz_build (mm), N_layers (count) | Process planning |
| 3 | **Part Volume (V)** | $V = N_{\text{layers}} \times A_{\text{layer}} \times h$ | m³ or cm³ | Material consumption |
| 4 | **Porosity (ε) of scaffold** | $\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_{\text{bulk}}}{\rho_{\text{solid}}}$ | ε dimensionless, ρ in g/cm³ | Bone scaffold design (target ε = 50–80%) |
| 5 | **Hatch Spacing Relation** | $h_{\text{pore}} \approx d_{\text{laser}} - s_{\text{hatch}}$ | d_laser = laser spot, s_hatch = hatch spacing | PBF process tuning |
| 6 | **Wenzel Roughness Factor** | $r = \dfrac{A_{\text{actual}}}{A_{\text{projected}}}$ | dimensionless | Cell adhesion enhancement |
| 7 | **Build Time (t_build)** | $t_{\text{build}} = \dfrac{h_{\text{total}}}{h_{\text{layer}}} \times t_{\text{layer}}$ | h_total = part height, t_layer = time/layer | Process planning |
| 8 | **Energy Density (ED)** | $ED = \dfrac{P}{v \times h \times s}$ | P = laser power (W), v = scan speed (mm/s), h = hatch spacing (mm), s = layer thickness (mm) | PBF process window |
| 9 | **Hencky Strain (bioprinting)** | $\varepsilon_H = -\ln\dfrac{h_{\text{filament}}}{d_{\text{nozzle}}}$ | dimensionless | Cell shear damage in extrusion |
| 10 | **Degradation half-life (PLA)** | $t_{1/2} \approx 6\text{–}24 \text{ months (in vivo)}$ | empirical | Drug delivery design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Density (ED) is the most-tested AM formula in KTU board exams.** Always quote the **units as J/mm³** and remember the typical **process window for Ti-6Al-4V in SLM is 60–120 J/mm³**. Below this → lack-of-fusion porosity; above this → keyhole porosity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in Biomaterials

1. **Patient-Specific Implants (PSI):** Convert DICOM (CT/MRI) data → STL → 3D-printed titanium mandible, cranial plate, or acetabular cup. Reduces surgery time by **30–50%**.
2. **Controlled Porosity Scaffolds:** AM enables **interconnected pore networks (200–500 µm)** for osteoconduction — impossible with traditional machining.
3. **Drug Delivery Devices:** 3D-printed tablets with **zero-order release kinetics** (constant drug release over time), e.g., Spritam® (levetiracetam).
4. **Surgical Planning Models:** Pre-operative rehearsal on anatomically accurate, patient-specific 3D-printed models reduces intra-operative complications.
5. **Functionally Graded Materials (FGM):** AM can vary composition layer-by-layer — e.g., porous Ti core + dense Ti cortical shell mimicking natural bone modulus gradient.
6. **Tissue Engineering & Regenerative Medicine:** Bioprinted skin (already in clinical trials for burns), cartilage, bone, and eventually organs.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between Layer Thickness, Build Time, and Part Height

**Problem Statement:** A PCL bone scaffold is to be fabricated by FDM with a total height of **H = 12 mm**. The printer extrudes one layer in time **t_layer = 8 s**, including material deposition, travel moves, and platform lowering. The chosen layer thickness is **h = 0.2 mm**. Compute:
(a) The total number of layers
(b) The total build time
(c) The volumetric build rate (VBR) if the average layer cross-sectional area is **A_layer = 50 mm²**

---

### Step 1: Compute the total number of layers

We begin from the **fundamental relationship** between part height, layer count, and layer thickness:

$$
N_{\text{layers}} = \frac{H_{\text{total}}}{h}
$$

where each symbol carries its standard physical meaning. Substituting the given numerical values:

$$
N_{\text{layers}} = \frac{12~\text{mm}}{0.2~\text{mm}} = 60~\text{layers}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 12 mm-tall scaffold requires **60 individual layers** to be deposited. Each layer must be cured/deposited before the next one is added, ensuring structural integrity of the final part.

---

### Step 2: Compute the total build time

The build time is a linear function of layer count and per-layer time:

$$
t_{\text{build}} = N_{\text{layers}} \times t_{\text{layer}}
$$

Inserting the previously computed value of N_layers and the per-layer time:

$$
t_{\text{build}} = 60 \times 8~\text{s} = 480~\text{s} = 8~\text{minutes}
$$

**Interpretation:** The total fabrication time is **8 minutes** — much faster than subtractive manufacturing of the same complex scaffold geometry. Note that this excludes post-processing time (UV curing, support removal, sterilization), which can add **30 min – 4 hours** depending on the biomaterial.

---

### Step 3: Compute the Volumetric Build Rate (VBR)

The volumetric build rate quantifies the material throughput of the AM system per unit time:

$$
\text{VBR} = \frac{V_{\text{part}}}{t_{\text{build}}} = \frac{A_{\text{layer}} \times H_{\text{total}}}{t_{\text{build}}}
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
\text{VBR} = \frac{50~\text{mm}^2 \times 12~\text{mm}}{480~\text{s}} = \frac{600~\text{mm}^3}{480~\text{s}}
$$

$$
\text{VBR} = 1.25~\text{mm}^3/\text{s} = 75~\text{mm}^3/\text{min} = 4.5~\text{cm}^3/\text{h}
$$

**Interpretation:** This is a typical VBR for laboratory/desktop FDM printers. Industrial SLS systems achieve **10–100 cm³/h**, while binder jetting can exceed **1000 cm³/h**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Density (ED) for SLM Process Window

**Problem Statement:** A biomedical engineer is fabricating a **Ti-6Al-4V acetabular cup** using Selective Laser Melting with the following process parameters:
- Laser power: **P = 200 W**
- Scan speed: **v = 800 mm/s**
- Hatch spacing: **s = 0.12 mm**
- Layer thickness: **h = 0.03 mm**

Compute the **volumetric energy density (ED)** in J/mm³, and determine whether the process is within the typical **process window (60–120 J/mm³)** for defect-free Ti-6Al-4V.

---

### Step 1: Recall the Energy Density Formula

The volumetric energy density is the laser energy delivered per unit volume of powder:

$$
ED = \frac{P}{v \cdot s \cdot h}
$$

where each term must be in **compatible SI-derived units** to yield **J/mm³**.

---

### Step 2: Dimensional Verification

Let us verify the units before substitution:

$$
ED = \frac{[W]}{[\text{mm/s}] \cdot [\text{mm}] \cdot [\text{mm}]} = \frac{[\text{J/s}]}{[\text{mm}^3/\text{s}]} = \frac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}^3}
$$

Confirmed: units are **J/mm³**. ✓

---

### Step 3: Substitute Numerical Values

$$
ED = \frac{200~\text{W}}{800~\frac{\text{mm}}{\text{s}} \times 0.12~\text{mm} \times 0.03~\text{mm}}
$$

Compute the denominator step-by-step:

$$
800 \times 0.12 = 96
$$

$$
96 \times 0.03 = 2.88
$$

Therefore:

$$
ED = \frac{200}{2.88} = 69.44~\text{J/mm}^3
$$

---

### Step 4: Compare Against the Process Window

The recommended energy density window for **Ti-6Al-4V in SLM** is:

$$
ED_{\text{window}} = 60 \text{ to } 120~\text{J/mm}^3
$$

Since:

$$
60 \leq 69.44 \leq 120
$$

the process is **WITHIN the optimal window** → fully dense part (> 99.5% theoretical density) with minimal lack-of-fusion defects.

---

### Step 5: Predictive Engineering Interpretation

- **If ED < 60 J/mm³** → insufficient melting → **lack-of-fusion porosity** (unmelted powder particles remain), mechanical weakness.
- **If ED > 120 J/mm³** → excessive energy → **keyhole porosity** (vaporization of metal creates gas pockets), balling, and surface roughness.
- **ED ≈ 70 J/mm³ (our result)** → balanced, dense part — ideal for load-bearing orthopedic implants.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Scaffold Porosity & Bone Tissue Ingrowth Pore Size

**Problem Statement:** A PCL scaffold is fabricated by FDM with the following bulk and solid densities:

- $\rho_{\text{bulk}} = 0.32~\text{g/cm}^3$ (measured by Archimedes)
- $\rho_{\text{solid}} = 1.09~\text{g/cm}^3$ (intrinsic PCL density)

Compute the **porosity (ε)** and verify whether it falls within the **osteoconductive window (50–80%)**.

---

### Step 1: Apply the Porosity Definition

$$
\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{\rho_{\text{bulk}}}{\rho_{\text{solid}}}
$$

---

### Step 2: Substitute Values

$$
\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{0.32}{1.09}
$$

Compute the ratio:

$$
\frac{0.32}{1.09} = 0.2936
$$

Therefore:

$$
\varepsilon = 1 - 0.2936 = 0.7064
$$

Convert to percentage:

$$
\varepsilon = 70.64\%
$$

---

### Step 3: Clinical Interpretation

Since **50% ≤ 70.64% ≤ 80%**, the scaffold falls **within the osteoconductive window** for bone tissue engineering. The recommended **pore size for bone ingrowth is 200–500 µm**, and the corresponding scaffold should have:
- **Interconnectivity > 95%** (no closed pores)
- **Compressive modulus 10–1500 MPa** (cancellous-to-cortical bone range)

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Exam Ready)

| Process | Resolution (µm) | Biocompatible Materials | Surface Finish | Cost | Best Biomedical Use |
|---------|------------------|------------------------|----------------|------|---------------------|
| **SLA** | **10–50** (highest) | Photopolymers, PEGDA, GelMA | Excellent (Ra < 1 µm) | Medium | Dental, hearing aids, micro-tissue scaffolds |
| **DLP** | **25–75** | Photopolymers | Excellent | Medium | Multi-material dental models |
| **FDM** | 100–250 | PCL, PLA, PLGA, PEEK | Moderate (layer lines) | **Lowest** | Surgical models, simple bone scaffolds |
| **SLS** | 80–200 | PA12, PEEK, HA-composite | Moderate (powder stuck) | High | Patient-specific implants, PEEK spinal cages |
| **SLM/DMLS** | 30–80 | Ti-6Al-4V, Co-Cr, 316L SS | Good (post-polish) | **Highest** | Load-bearing orthopedic implants |
| **EBM** | 100–200 | Ti-6Al-4V, Co-Cr | Rougher (powder) | Highest | Acetabular cups, large orthopedic |
| **3DP (Binder Jet)** | 100–300 | Ceramics + binder, drug powders | Porous (needs infiltration) | Medium | Drug tablets, ceramic bone scaffolds |
| **PolyJet** | **14–28** (highest) | Photopolymers, rubber-like | Excellent | Medium-High | Multi-material anatomical models |
| **Bioprinting (extrusion)** | 100–500 | Hydrogels + cells | Moderate | Medium | Tissue engineering, organoids |
| **Bioprinting (inkjet)** | 50–200 | Low-viscosity bioinks | Good | Medium | Skin, cartilage patches |
| **Bioprinting (laser/LIFT)** | **10–100** (best cell viability) | Any bioink | Excellent | **Highest** | High-density cell constructs |

---

## 3.5 Critical Engineering Trade-Off — The "AM Triangle"

A fundamental concept every KTU student must internalize — the **AM Triangle of Trade-offs**:

$$
\text{RESOLUTION} \longleftrightarrow \text{BUILD SPEED} \longleftrightarrow \text{MATERIAL PROPERTIES}
$$

**You can only optimize TWO of these three simultaneously:**

- High resolution + fast build speed → poor mechanical strength (e.g., micro-SLA with low-molecular-weight resin)
- High resolution + good mechanical properties → slow build (e.g., EBM with thick layers, or SLM with fine parameters)
- Fast build speed + good mechanical properties → coarse resolution (e.g., binder jetting)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** When asked *"Which AM process is best for X?"*, always justify using the **AM Triangle** — and quote a specific quantitative trade-off (e.g., *"SLA offers 25 µm resolution but limits materials to photopolymers"*).

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic AM Process Workflow (DICOM-to-Implant Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient CT/MRI Scan] --> B[DICOM Data Extraction]
    B --> C[Segmentation Software Mimics or 3D Slicer]
    C --> D[STL Mesh Generation Triangulation]
    D --> E[Slicing Software Cura or Magics]
    E --> F[Layer-by-Layer G-code]
    F --> G[AM Machine SLA, FDM, EBM, etc.]
    G --> H[Green Part 3D Printed Scaffold]
    H --> I[Post-Processing Support Removal, Curing, Sintering]
    I --> J[Surface Treatment Sterilization, Coating]
    J --> K[Quality Assurance Dimensional and Biocompatibility]
    K --> L[Patient-Specific Implant Ready for Surgery]
```

## 4.2 ASTM F2792 AM Process Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Additive Manufacturing ASTM F2792 Seven Categories]
    ROOT --> VP[Vat Photopolymerization]
    ROOT --> ME[Material Extrusion]
    ROOT --> PBF[Powder Bed Fusion]
    ROOT --> MJ[Material Jetting]
    ROOT --> BJ[Binder Jetting]
    ROOT --> SL[Sheet Lamination]
    ROOT --> DED[Directed Energy Deposition]

    VP --> VP1[SLA - UV Laser Scan]
    VP --> VP2[DLP - UV Projector]

    ME --> ME1[FDM - Filament Extrusion]
    ME --> ME2[DIW - Direct Ink Writing]
    ME --> ME3[Robocasting]

    PBF --> PBF1[SLS - Laser Sinter]
    PBF --> PBF2[SLM - Laser Melt]
    PBF --> PBF3[DMLS - Laser Melt]
    PBF --> PBF4[EBM - Electron Beam]

    MJ --> MJ1[PolyJet - Photopolymer Jetting]
    MJ --> MJ2[Inkjet - Droplet]

    BJ --> BJ1[3DP - Powder Binder]
    BJ --> BJ2[Metal Binder Jetting]

    SL --> SL1[LOM - Laser Cut Sheets]
    SL --> SL2[UAM - Ultrasonic Welding]

    DED --> DED1[LENS - Laser Cladding]
    DED --> DED2[EBAM - Electron Beam]
    DED --> DED3[WAAM - Wire Arc]
```

## 4.3 FDM Functional Architecture (Block-Level Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FIL[Filament Spool PCL or PLA] --> EXT[Heated Liquefier 180-250 deg C]
    EXT --> NZ[Nozzle Diameter 0.2-0.4 mm]
    NZ --> BD[Build Platform Heated Bed]
    BD --> LY[Layer 1 Deposit and Cool]
    LY --> LY2[Layer 2 Lower Platform and Deposit]
    LY2 --> LY3[Layer N Continue Stacking]
    LY3 --> POST[Post-Processing UV Cure, Sterilize]
    POST --> FINAL[Final PCL Scaffold]

    CTRL[G-code Controller] -.->|Toolpath| EXT
    CTRL -.->|Z-axis step| BD
```

## 4.4 SLA Functional Architecture (Block-Level Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RESIN[Photopolymer Resin Vat PEGDA + Photoinitiator] --> SURF[Resin Surface Free Level]
    SURF --> LASER[UV Laser Lambda 355 nm HeCd or Solid-State]
    LASER --> SCAN[Galvanometer Scanner XY Direction]
    SCAN --> CURE[Localized Photopolymerization Single Layer]
    CURE --> DOWN[Build Platform Lowers by h = 10-50 microns]
    DOWN --> BLADE[Recoater Blade Sweeps Fresh Resin]
    BLADE --> CURE
    CURE --> POSTC[Post-Cure UV Oven 10-30 min]
    POSTC --> FINAL[Final SLA Part]
```

## 4.5 EBM Functional Architecture (Block-Level Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    POWD[Ti-6Al-4V Powder Reservoir] --> ROLL[Roller Spreads Powder Layer 50-100 microns]
    ROLL --> BED[Build Plate Preheated to 700 deg C]
    BED --> EB[Electron Beam 60 kV, 3-15 kW]
    EB --> COIL[Electromagnetic Coils Steer Beam]
    COIL --> MELT[Selective Melting of Metal Powder]
    MELT --> DOWN[Build Plate Lowers One Layer]
    DOWN --> ROLL
    MELT --> COOL[Cool in Vacuum to Below 100 deg C]
    COOL --> POWDER[Remove Unmelted Powder by Blasting]
    POWDER --> FINAL[Finished Ti-6Al-4V Implant]
```

## 4.6 Bioprinting Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Technique

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START{What is Your Target Application?}
    START -->|High cell viability needed| LIFT[Laser-Assisted Bioprinting LIFT]
    START -->|High mechanical integrity| EXTR[Extrusion-Based Bioprinting]
    START -->|High throughput cell deposition| INKJ[Inkjet Bioprinting]
    START -->|Vascular channels| SACR[Sacrificial Bioprinting]

    LIFT --> L1[Resolution 10-100 microns]
    L1 --> L2[Cell Viability above 95 percent]
    L2 --> L3[Nozzle-Free, No Shear Damage]

    EXTR --> E1[Resolution 100-1000 microns]
    E1 --> E2[Cell Viability 40-80 percent]
    E2 --> E3[High Viscosity Bioinks Possible]

    INKJ --> I1[Resolution 50-200 microns]
    I1 --> I2[Cell Viability above 85 percent]
    I2 --> I3[Low Viscosity Required below 10 mPa.s]

    SACR --> S1[Print Sacrificial Material Pluronic or Gelatin]
    S1 --> S2[Liquefy to Form Channels]
    S2 --> S3[Vascularized Tissue Construct]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**Define Additive Manufacturing. List any four ASTM F2792 process categories with one biomedical application each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Additive Manufacturing (AM) is defined by **ASTM F2792** as *"a process of joining materials to make objects from 3D model data, usually layer upon layer, as opposed to subtractive manufacturing methodologies."* It is also known as Rapid Prototyping (RP), Solid Freeform Fabrication (SFF), or 3D Printing.

Four ASTM F2792 categories with biomedical applications:

| # | Process Category | Biomedical Application |
|---|------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Vat Photopolymerization (SLA/DLP)** | Dental crowns, hearing aid shells |
| 2 | **Material Extrusion (FDM)** | PCL bone tissue engineering scaffolds |
| 3 | **Powder Bed Fusion (SLS/EBM/SLM)** | Ti-6Al-4V acetabular cups, PEEK spinal cages |
| 4 | **Binder Jetting (3DP)** | Drug tablets (Spritam®/levetiracetam), ceramic bone scaffolds |

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for correctly listing 4 categories with valid applications. Examiner tip: must use ASTM terminology.)*

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**Differentiate between SLA and FDM with respect to: (i) working principle, (ii) biomaterial used, (iii) one advantage and one limitation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | SLA (Stereolithography) | FDM (Fused Deposition Modeling) |
|-----------|--------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **(i) Working Principle** | UV laser selectively cures a vat of liquid photopolymer resin layer by layer. | Heated nozzle extrudes molten thermoplastic filament, which solidifies on cooling. |
| **(ii) Biomaterial Used** | Photopolymers — PEGDA, GelMA, bio-resins | Thermoplastics — PCL, PLA, PLGA, PEEK |
| **(iii) Advantage** | Highest resolution (≤ 25 µm), excellent surface finish | Low cost, simple, wide material range, multi-material capable |
| **(iii) Limitation** | Limited to photopolymers; post-curing needed; residual monomer toxicity | Layer-line artifacts, anisotropic properties, thermal degradation of sensitive biopolymers |

*(1 mark per row, 3 rows = 3 marks. Examiner expects a TABULAR comparison.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**With neat schematic, explain the working of Stereolithography (SLA) process. Discuss its advantages and limitations for biomedical applications.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
SLA is a **Vat Photopolymerization** process in which a vat of **photopolymer resin** is selectively cured by a **UV laser (λ = 355 nm)**. The laser traces the cross-sectional geometry of each layer, causing **free-radical photopolymerization** of monomers (e.g., (meth)acrylates, PEGDA) into a crosslinked solid network. The build platform is submerged just below the resin surface, and after each layer is cured, it lowers by one layer thickness (**10–50 µm**). A **recoater blade** sweeps a fresh layer of liquid resin, and the next layer is cured. The process continues until the full 3D part is built, after which **post-curing in a UV oven** completes the polymerization.

**Schematic (block-level description for drawing):**
- Resin vat (bottom-up configuration)
- Build platform (motorized Z-axis)
- UV laser source
- Galvanometer-based X-Y scanner
- Recoater blade
- Controller/computer

**Advantages for Biomedical Use (2 marks):**
1. **Highest resolution** (≤ 25 µm XY) — suitable for **microfluidic devices**, scaffolds with micro-features, and dental restorations.
2. **Excellent surface finish** (Ra < 1 µm) — favourable for cell adhesion and **biocompatibility**.
3. Wide range of **biocompatible photopolymers** (PEGDA, GelMA) for **tissue engineering scaffolds** with controlled crosslinking density.
4. Used for **patient-specific surgical guides** in orthognathic surgery and dental implantology.

**Limitations for Biomedical Use (1 mark):**
1. Limited to **photopolymers** — cannot directly print metals or ceramics.
2. **Residual monomer and photoinitiator** leaching is a **biocompatibility concern** — requires thorough post-curing.
3. **Toxic uncured resin** requires careful handling.
4. Most SLA resins are **NOT FDA-approved for long-term implants** (only for models/guides).

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the FDM process for fabricating biopolymer scaffolds. Discuss the role of process parameters (nozzle temperature, layer thickness, infill density) on scaffold properties.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**FDM Process for Biopolymer Scaffolds (3 marks):**
Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) is a **Material Extrusion** process in which a **thermoplastic biopolymer filament** (e.g., PCL, PLA, PLGA) is fed into a **heated liquefier** maintained above the polymer's **melting temperature** (T_m). The molten polymer is extruded through a fine nozzle (typically **0.1–0.4 mm**) and deposited along the **computer-generated toolpath** of each sliced layer. The polymer solidifies upon cooling, bonding with the previously deposited layer. By controlling the deposition pattern, **interconnected porous 3D scaffolds** with **controlled pore architecture** are built layer-by-layer for **bone and cartilage tissue engineering**.

**Role of Process Parameters (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Effect on Scaffold Property |
|-----------|----------------------------|
| **Nozzle Temperature (T_n)** | Too low → poor interlayer bonding, weak mechanical strength. Too high → thermal degradation of biopolymer (chain scission), reduced molecular weight, faster degradation, release of acidic byproducts. Optimal T_n for PCL = 80–120 °C. |
| **Layer Thickness (h)** | Smaller h → higher resolution, smoother surface, longer build time, better mechanical strength (more interlayer contact). Larger h → faster build, rougher surface, potential weak interlayer adhesion. |
| **Infill Density (%)** | Higher infill → denser scaffold, higher mechanical strength, lower porosity (poor nutrient diffusion). Lower infill → higher porosity (>70%), better cell ingrowth, but weaker mechanically. Optimal for bone TE = 50–80% porosity with 200–500 µm pore size. |
| **Print Speed (v)** | Higher v → faster build but possible under-extrusion, weaker bonds. Lower v → better bonding, longer build time. |
| **Build Orientation** | Affects **anisotropic mechanical properties** — load-bearing direction should align with strongest fiber direction. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Using vague terms** like "3D printing" instead of ASTM-standard "Additive Manufacturing / FDM / SLA" → **−0.5 to −1 mark**.
> 2. **Missing a labeled schematic** in SLA/FDM questions → **−1 to −2 marks**. Always draw a *labeled* block diagram.
> 3. **Conflating SLA with SLS** (UV laser vs CO₂ laser; photopolymer vat vs powder bed) → **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Not mentioning ASTM F2792** when defining AM → **−0.5 mark**.
> 5. **Omitting biocompatibility considerations** (residual monomer, post-curing, sterilization) in biomedical AM questions → **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 6. **Wrong units** for energy density (e.g., W/mm³ instead of J/mm³) → **−0.5 mark**.
> 7. **In bioprinting questions**, forgetting to mention the **resolution vs. cell viability trade-off** → **−1 mark**.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) process. Compare SLS with Electron Beam Melting (EBM) for fabrication of metallic orthopedic implants.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) — Working (3 marks):**
SLS is a **Powder Bed Fusion** process in which a **CO₂ laser (λ = 10.6 µm)** or **fiber laser** selectively scans and **sinters** (heats to **0.7–0.9 T_m**, below the melting point) a thin layer of powder spread by a roller or blade on a build platform. After each layer is sintered, the platform lowers by one layer thickness (**100–150 µm**), a fresh powder layer is spread, and the laser sinters the next cross-section. The **unsintered powder acts as a built-in support**, eliminating the need for sacrificial support structures — a major advantage for complex internal geometries. After the build, the part is cooled and excess powder is removed by brushing or blasting.

**Comparison Table — SLS vs. EBM for Metallic Orthopedic Implants (4 marks):**

| Parameter | SLS (Selective Laser Sintering) | EBM (Electron Beam Melting) |
|-----------|----------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Energy Source** | CO₂ or fiber laser | High-energy electron beam (60 kV, 3–15 kW) |
| **Working Atmosphere** | Inert gas (N₂) — to prevent oxidation | **High vacuum (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ mbar)** |
| **Build Plate Temperature** | Pre-heated to ~170–200 °C | Pre-heated to **~700 °C** (reduces residual stress) |
| **Beam Steering** | Galvanometer mirrors (optical) | Electromagnetic deflection coils |
| **Typical Materials** | PA12, PEEK, HA-composite, some Ti | **Ti-6Al-4V**, Co-Cr, Ti-Grade 2 |
| **Surface Finish** | Moderate (powder adhesion) | Rougher than SLM |
| **Build Speed** | Moderate | **Faster** (high power, high pre-heat) |
| **Residual Stress** | Moderate | **Lower** (high build temp anneals in situ) |
| **Orthopedic Use** | PEEK spinal cages, HA bone scaffolds | **Ti acetabular cups**, CMF plates, tumor prostheses |
| **FDA-Approved Examples** | OsteoFab (Oxford Performance Materials) PEEK cranial implant | **ARCLINE (Smith & Nephew)** acetabular cup |

**Conclusion:** Both SLS and EBM are capable of fabricating metallic orthopedic implants, but **EBM is preferred for large, load-bearing Ti-6Al-4V components** (e.g., hip cups) due to lower residual stress and faster build, while **SLS is preferred for PEEK and bioceramic scaffolds** where laser-polymer interaction is gentler.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Discuss the role of Additive Manufacturing in (i) patient-specific implants, (ii) tissue engineering scaffolds, and (iii) drug delivery devices. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) Patient-Specific Implants (2.5 marks):**
AM enables fabrication of implants that precisely match the patient's anatomy, derived from **CT/MRI DICOM data → STL → 3D print**. This eliminates the need for intra-operative manual shaping, reduces surgery time by **30–50%**, and improves implant fit. Customized **Ti-6Al-4V cranial plates** (e.g., by **OsteoFab, Oxford Performance Materials**) and **mandibular reconstruction plates** are clinically approved examples. AM also allows controlled **surface texturing** (gyroid, lattices) for **osseointegration**.

**(ii) Tissue Engineering Scaffolds (2.5 marks):**
AM scaffolds are fabricated with **controlled pore size (200–500 µm)**, **interconnectivity > 95%**, and **porosity 50–80%** to mimic the **extracellular matrix (ECM)** and support cell attachment, proliferation, and differentiation. FDM-printed **PCL/PLA scaffolds** with **hydroxyapatite (HA) coating** are used for **bone regeneration**, while bioprinted **alginate/GelMA hydrogels** with encapsulated stem cells are used for **cartilage** and **skin** tissue engineering.

**(iii) Drug Delivery Devices (2 marks):**
AM enables fabrication of **complex-release drug tablets and implants** with **multi-drug loading**, **patient-specific dosing**, and **complex internal architectures** (e.g., **gyroid, fractal, compartmentalized structures**) that are impossible with conventional tablet pressing. The landmark example is **Spritam® (levetiracetam)** by Aprecia Pharmaceuticals (FDA-approved 2015), the **first 3D-printed drug**, manufactured using **ZipDose® binder jetting technology** to create a highly porous tablet that disintegrates rapidly with a sip of water — beneficial for **epileptic patients** with dysphagia. AM also enables **personalized polypills** for multi-drug regimens in geriatric care.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4: Additive Manufacturing**

### **Core Definitions**
- ☐ **ASTM F2792** definition of AM — "joining materials layer by layer from 3D model data"
- ☐ **Chuck Hull (1984)** — inventor of SLA, founder of 3D Systems
- ☐ AM = RP = SFF = 3DP (synonymous in KTU context)

### **Seven ASTM F2792 Categories (must memorize)**
- ☐ Vat Photopolymerization, Material Extrusion, Powder Bed Fusion, Material Jetting, Binder Jetting, Sheet Lamination, Directed Energy Deposition
- ☐ Mnemonic: **"VMP-MBSD"** (Very Many People Make Beautiful Sculptures Directly)

### **Process-to-Application Mapping**
- ☐ **SLA** → photopolymers, dental, hearing aids, micro-scaffolds (best resolution)
- ☐ **FDM** → PCL/PLA scaffolds, surgical models (lowest cost, most popular)
- ☐ **SLS** → PEEK spinal cages, HA bone scaffolds, surgical models
- ☐ **SLM/DMLS** → Ti-6Al-4V orthopedic implants (load-bearing)
- ☐ **EBM** → Ti acetabular cups, large CMF plates (fastest metal AM)
- ☐ **3DP Binder Jetting** → Spritam® drug tablet, ceramic scaffolds
- ☐ **Bioprinting** → tissue engineering, organoids, regenerative medicine

### **Key Numerical Values to Memorize**
- ☐ SLA resolution: **10–50 µm** (best)
- ☐ FDM resolution: **100–250 µm**
- ☐ SLM Ti-6Al-4V energy density: **60–120 J/mm³** (process window)
- ☐ Bone scaffold porosity: **50–80%**, pore size: **200–500 µm**
- ☐ SLA layer thickness: **10–50 µm**; SLS: **100–150 µm**; FDM: **50–400 µm**
- ☐ T_g of PLA: ~55–60 °C; T_g of PCL: ~−60 °C; T_g of PEEK: ~143 °C
- ☐ **Spritam® (levetiracetam)** — first FDA-approved 3D-printed drug, **2015**

### **Critical Formulas**
- ☐ $\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_{\text{bulk}}}{\rho_{\text{solid}}}$ (porosity)
- ☐ $ED = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot s \cdot h}$ (energy density, J/mm³)
- ☐ $N_{\text{layers}} = \dfrac{H_{\text{total}}}{h}$ (build planning)
- ☐ $t_{\text{build}} = N_{\text{layers}} \times t_{\text{layer}}$ (build time)

### **Engineering Trade-Offs to Remember**
- ☐ **AM Triangle:** Resolution ↔ Speed ↔ Material Properties (only 2 of 3 optimizable)
- ☐ **Bioprinting:** Resolution inversely proportional to cell viability
- ☐ **EBM vs SLM:** EBM is faster but rougher; SLM is slower but smoother
- ☐ **Sintering vs Melting:** Sintering (T < T_m) leaves porosity; Melting (T ≥ T_m) gives full density
- ☐ **Powder bed = self-supporting**; vat/FDM/PolyJet = need support structures

### **Biomedical-Specific Considerations (Always Mention in Answers)**
- ☐ **Biocompatibility** of material AND process (residual monomer, photoinitiator, powder residue)
- ☐ **Sterilization compatibility** (autoclave vs. EtO vs. γ-radiation)
- ☐ **Mechanical anisotropy** due to layer-by-layer construction
- ☐ **Surface roughness effect** on cell adhesion (Wenzel roughness factor)
- ☐ **Regulatory pathway:** FDA 510(k) for patient-specific implants based on CT/MRI

### **Frequently Asked Comparison Questions (Practice These)**
- ☐ SLA vs FDM (process, material, advantage, limitation)
- ☐ SLS vs EBM (energy source, atmosphere, material, application)
- ☐ Sintering vs Melting (mechanism, density, defect type)
- ☐ Bioprinting: Extrusion vs Inkjet vs Laser (viability, resolution, viscosity)

### **Highest-Yield Single-Line Exam Facts**
- ☐ "AM is the opposite of subtractive manufacturing"
- ☐ "DICOM data → STL mesh → slicing → G-code → layer-by-layer build"
- ☐ "EBM works in vacuum; SLM works in inert gas atmosphere"
- ☐ "SLS does not require support structures because unsintered powder supports the part"
- ☐ "FDM is the lowest-cost and most widely accessible AM process"
- ☐ "SLA offers the highest resolution among commercial AM processes"
